title: Helping Small Businesses THRIVE Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new program to help small businesses deal with rising costs of things they need to run their companies. It lets small businesses make special agreements to lock in prices for important items like gasoline and diesel fuel. This way, small businesses can protect themselves from sudden price increases that might hurt their ability to operate. The program is designed to help small businesses stay stable even when prices for basic goods go up quickly.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Helping Small Businesses To Hedge Risk and Insure against Volatile Expenses Act or the Helping Small Businesses THRIVE Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, the following words mean specific things. Administrator means the person who runs the Small Business Administration. Commission, commodity, commodity pool, commodity trading advisor, future delivery, and futures commission merchant have the same meanings as defined in section one a of the Commodity Exchange Act. Covered commodity means any raw material or basic good that the Administrator decides can be part of agreements made under section four part a. Eligible entity means a small business that qualifies for this program. However, it does not include small businesses that are financial institutions like banks, businesses already regulated by the Commodity Exchange Commission for financial activities, investment advisers that must register with the Securities and Exchange Commission, brokers as defined in securities law, businesses that have been operating for less than one year when they apply, or businesses that the Administrator decides should be excluded to keep the program focused on its main purpose of helping small businesses manage their direct operating costs. Program means the Helping Small Businesses Thrive Program created under section three part a. Resource partners means small business development centers, women's business centers, chapters of the Service Corps of Retired Executives, and Veteran Business Outreach Centers. Small business concern and small business development center have the same meanings as defined in section three of the Small Business Act.

sections.title: Section 3  Helping Small Businesses Thrive Program
sections.text: The Administrator must create a test program within one year after this law is passed. The Administrator will work with the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, the Secretary of the Treasury, and other appropriate federal officials to set up this program. The program will be called the Helping Small Businesses Thrive Program. Its purpose is to help qualifying small businesses reduce the risk they face when the prices of raw materials and basic goods they need for their business go up. Small businesses that want to join the program must submit an application. The application must be submitted at the time, in the way, and with the information that the Administrator decides is needed. The application must include information that proves the business qualifies for the program. It may also include extra information to help the Administrator properly assist the business in deciding whether joining the program would help them, including a description of expenses the business has related to commodities. The Administrator must create guidance and post it on a public Small Business Administration website. This guidance will help qualifying businesses decide whether they should apply to join the program and whether the program would help them. The guidance will include information about the program's purpose, what products it offers, and how those products can reduce price risk for businesses when commodity prices change. It will also include information about determining commodity costs, business expenses related to each covered commodity, when commodity expenses might be too high for the program to be helpful, what percentage of commodity expenses are best to protect through the program, and how different types of business revenue models might affect participation. The Administrator must reach out to small businesses to share information about the program and its benefits. This includes providing materials to small business centers, stakeholders and trade associations, and resource partners to give to small businesses. The Administrator will also conduct online seminars or in person events about the program and operate a website and phone line that offers more information and allows businesses to ask questions and get help deciding if the program would benefit them. When running the program, the Administrator can make necessary rules and work with the Commission to form a commodity pool and apply to register as a commodity pool operator under the Commodity Exchange Act. The Administrator cannot take physical delivery of any actual commodity except in extreme emergency situations. The Administrator must buy and sell as needed to close positions with covered commodities to make sure they follow this rule. Congress authorizes the appropriation of whatever money is needed for the Administrator to create and operate the program. This money will remain available until five years after this law is passed.

sections.title: Section 4  Assisting Small Businesses Transacting in Commodity Futures Markets
sections.text: The Administrator must make agreements with qualifying businesses that have been accepted into the program. These agreements will help those businesses buy and sell in commodity futures markets for any commodity the Administrator selects. A qualifying business can make one or more agreements where they agree to purchase a covered commodity or a related financial product at a price set by the Administrator for the length of the agreement. When deciding which agreements to offer to a business, the Administrator must consider how closely the agreement matches the actual costs of the business and whether an agreement already offered provides similar benefits, how to keep things simple for the business, how to reduce trading costs, and how to minimize the number and type of market positions the program holds to reduce costs and potential errors. Each agreement must offer the covered commodity or related financial product to the business at cost, including any fees and commissions the Administrator pays to get the commodity or financial product. The Administrator can offer call option purchase agreements through the program to protect businesses when the price of a covered commodity goes up by more than five percent. Each agreement must clearly state that the business is responsible for all costs associated with the agreement and that any initial costs must be paid when the business enters into the agreement. Agreements must last at least sixty days but no more than three years. The Administrator must ensure that most agreements last at least one hundred twenty days. The Administrator decides which commodities can be part of agreements. The Administrator must make sure that gasoline and diesel gasoline are covered commodities. During the first year after the program starts, the Administrator can offer agreements for no more than three additional covered commodities besides gasoline and diesel gasoline, and only one of these can be intended for a specific industry use. The Administrator can only remove a commodity from being eligible after giving at least ninety days notice to program participants. The Administrator must use contracts available through entities regulated by the Commission, like designated contract markets, as much as possible. When deciding on additional commodities or removing commodities, the Administrator will work with the Commission and consider feedback from businesses and stakeholders including survey data about demand for adding commodities to the list, demand and transaction volume in particular commodities, available liquidity in new commodity markets, and the program's capacity regarding funding and staff expertise. The Administrator will give special consideration to classifying standard utilities like electricity and natural gas as covered commodities. The Administrator must conduct or help with transactions in commodity derivatives markets that are necessary to fulfill the program's obligations under agreements with businesses. The Administrator can make an agreement with a commodity trading advisor or futures commission merchant to carry out these transactions. The Administrator must use any money earned by the program in a fiscal year to offset the program's operating costs for that year. Any money beyond what is needed for operating costs must be returned to the general fund of the Treasury.

sections.title: Section 5  Reports
sections.text: Within one hundred twenty days after this law is passed, the Administrator must submit a report to the Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship of the Senate and the Committee on Small Business of the House of Representatives. This report must include a description of the program's structure and procedures including how covered commodities are selected, a plan for managing the program, and a description of the merit based review process used to select businesses to participate in the program. Starting one year after this law is passed and every year after that, the Administrator must submit annual reports to the same committees. These reports must include the number of applications submitted by businesses for participation in the program, the number of agreements made under section four part a, the total notional value of covered commodities that were the subjects of those agreements broken down by covered commodity, and the effect of the program on participating businesses including feedback from those businesses about any costs and benefits of the program regarding their business operations, especially expansion and growth plans. When collecting information from businesses for the annual reports, the Administrator cannot require a business to report more often than when their agreement ends or annually, whichever is less frequent. However, for the first two fiscal years after a business enters into an agreement, the Administrator cannot require reporting more often than when the agreement ends or annually, whichever is more frequent.
